story:
Harlow hovered over the glistening inlet, wings buzzing with excitement as she watched for any ripple on the calm sea. “They’re here, Oakley! I can see their tails!” she sang, circling her older sister. Oakley, composed and careful, felt the same flutter of wonder but kept her gaze cool as two mermaid sisters surfaced at the mouth of the cove.

Emma, the elder mermaid with long flaming red hair and an emerald tail that shimmered like the sea, rose first. Little Peyton, auburn curls wet against her cheeks, popped up beside her. “I told you, Emma! I told you they would come!” Peyton gasped. “All right, catch your breath,” Emma soothed.

The littlest ones met as if they’d known each other forever. Peyton flipped in the shallows; Harlow cartwheeled through the air, laughter bouncing between sea and sky. Meanwhile, the older sisters watched with cautious curiosity. Oakley, best archer in her class and keen of eye, studied Emma’s regal poise. Her tail gleamed so perfectly with the water that only someone like Oakley could trace its outline. “She is beautiful, poised, like a princess of the sea,” Oakley decided. Emma, in turn, took in the fairy: long sun-gold-streaked hair, strong legs, and purple wings that seemed to catch every color of light. How could wings move so fast?

The sun slipped toward the horizon. “Time to go, little one,” Oakley called. “You know how Mama is about the sun going to sleep in the ocean.” Harlow pleaded for more time. Across the water, Emma beckoned Peyton, “Before Papa has the whole fleet looking for us.” Reluctantly, the younger sisters returned to their guardians’ sides.

Before they parted, Emma met Oakley’s eyes. “Promise you won’t tell anyone about us. We’re forbidden to come to this side of the island.” Oakley nodded; they were beyond their own boundaries, too. “Pinky promise!” Peyton chirped, thrusting a small finger toward Harlow. Harlow linked hers without hesitation. Oakley and Emma exchanged a startled look. A pinky promise didn’t belong to mermaids or fairies—it belonged to humans.

They separated, the littles chattering, the elders thinking. Harlow spiraled around Oakley, breathless with questions—weren’t the mermaids beautiful? Could they meet again tomorrow? Oakley barely heard her. In fairy lore, an old tale told of a human boy who met a fairy princess and, to seal their secret friendship, taught her the pinky promise. How did Peyton know it? Emma swam while Peyton bubbled about flying high. Emma recalled her own version: when she turned ten, her father told of a human boy and a mermaid princess and the sacred oath of pinky fingers. Peyton was too young to know the legend—yet she did. And Oakley had recognized it too. How could a fairy and a mermaid share a human secret neither world was supposed to touch?

The sisters reached home before questions could rise. Harlow whirled around their room, pure joy and motion. Oakley, always the born protector, was quiet and thoughtful. Somewhere far below, Emma settled her little sister, the same wondering weight in her chest.

Both older girls sensed their story had only begun. There was magic on land and secrets in the sea waiting to be found. That night, both dreamed the same dream: a cave opening to a green meadow bright with flowers, a waterfall spilling into a crystal pond, the air full of laughter. In that place, tails and wings were set aside, and they ran on two legs like human children. Tomorrow would bring adventure—and answers—all sealed with a pinky promise.
